titleStmt.title: Introduction to the Site of Huangshi Cliff

div.head: 黄石崖

figure.head: Yellow Stone Cliff seen from the north; photograph taken in 2012
黄石崖北崖遠景。2012年拍攝

div.p: 黄石崖爲山東境内最北且最早的刻經址。此處刻經爲兩部佛經中三段節文的結合。刻經位於一排造像佛龕以及銘文之間。事實上，刻經址的群像使單塊方形的刻銘相形見絀。然而刻經已不復存。
山東省境内另有三處刻經已佚。不同於尖、陽和嵫陽山，黄石崖刻經非因採石所毀，而是惡意人爲。此事約發生於1894年之前某時。所幸此處刻經尚存於金石學家的記録中，勤奮的金石學家吴式芬（1796－1856）即屬其一。一張拓本仍存世，後人以此可得見此處刻經的行文、佈局及書法。
刻經無確切紀年，著録記載其位置處於一則紀年爲北魏孝昌二年（526）的造像題記之下。顯然，此刻經要比紀年爲北齊皇建元年（560）的山東早期刻經早三十年以上。時間差距解釋了黄石崖刻經書法風格的不同，此風格代表石刻佛經發展的一個短暫階段。此處尚未形成獨立成熟的摩崖刻經，刻經亦非刻於窟壁之上。以河北省響堂山爲例，刻經與造像相伴而存，亦出現於公元6世紀60年代。
八則造像題記提供了活動於此的供養人的豐富信息。其中有幾位社會高階層人物，他們與洛陽有密切關係。此時，龍門正沐浴於皇恩之下，開山造像伊始。

div.head: 地理位置
div.p: 黄石崖位於省會濟南市南郊，歷下區西南邊，螺絲頂山主峰的北麓。其地理位置是東經117.0356度，北緯36.6302度，相當於UTM坐標系統第50S帶，東行503188，北行4053851。螺絲頂山，亦稱爲羅袁寺頂或羅庵寺頂，位於海拔350米處。其東南邊緊鄰撅子山，東邊爲羊頭山和平頂山。西北側爲千佛山的一個山谷所分隔，市政旅遊公路的南段沿谷而設（）。新近修砌的石階小徑對攀登黄石崖佛教遺址助益頗大。
今濟南市在北朝時期行政區劃變動較大，《水經注·濟水》：“濟水又東北，濼水入焉，水出歷城縣故城西南，泉源上奮，水湧若輪。……城南對山，山上有舜祠，山下有大穴，謂之舜井……其水北爲大明湖，西即大明寺……。”《水經注校證》第199－200頁。可知其城市核心區基本在今濟南市區中心，黄石崖的造像區距離當時的市區不遠。

div.head: 地形與空間構成
div.p: 黄石崖斷崖處下部有一寬3至5米左右的平台，造像區沿平台横向延伸35米，高10米左右。其東部有一天然岩洞（黄石崖2號窟），洞内雕刻造像。另有眾多小龕主要分佈在該洞西側的崖面。1995年，仍存有25座小型佛龕（），加上天然岩洞内的造像，共有佛、菩薩像86尊。此數據不含背光中的化佛和飛天，以及當時所見隱匿於崖面落石下的幾尊小佛像。次年初，20號、21號、22號、24號與25號龕以及另一座無造像的空龕崩毀（）。現今崖壁下的山坳仍能發現崩落的造像殘石。這一帶岩石疏鬆，造像完成以來可能遭遇多次塌落。常盤大定、関野貞刊布的遺址全景照片攝於1921年，常盤大定和関野貞第7輯：圖版43，圖1。當時山上植被不佳，尚能明顯地看到造像所在區域有大片山岩脱落，大量碎石沿山坳傾瀉而下。歷史上山體的自然崩塌和人爲破壞情况並無詳細的記載，造像群原初的規模應更大些。
選自《大般涅槃經》的兩段偈語和《妙法蓮華經》闡釋觀世音名節文（黄石崖1號）合刻於黄石崖。此刻經面積高38釐米，寬41釐米，共12行（）。但該刻經未能倖存。根據吴式芬《金石彙目分編》所載，此刻經位於孝昌二年（526）造像題記（本卷黄石崖3號）之下：
div.quote: 在孝昌二年造像記下，西偏，石坎，高廣與記同，當同時刻。《金石彙目分編》第21101頁。
div.p: 八則造像題記（本卷黄石崖2號至9號）發現於此刻經址。其中六則，黄石崖2號、4號、5號、7號、8號和9號仍在原處（和）。黄石崖3號和6號所刻僅存拓本。1892年一名爲李剩仔的拓片販子見本卷鄭岩“黄石崖──佛教石窟在山東地區的傳播”一文，。又見下文“討論”部分，。蓄意毀壞東魏興和元年（539）由乞伏鋭所捐造的黄石崖6號。黄石崖3號所在的岩塊亦於1996年崩壞。據早年尋訪此處的張總和岡田健説，崖壁西面仍可見殘刻。即張總第37頁，圖1，刻銘G。岡田健第79頁，圖4。

div.head: 地名
div.p: 黄石崖山體呈亮灰，岩質爲輕微泛黄的寒武紀時期形成的石灰石，因而得名“黄石崖”。

figure.head: Yellow Stone Cliff seen from the west. Sketch by F. S. Drake; published in 196, fig. 2
黄石崖西面示意圖，林仰山繪製。刊於林仰山第196頁，圖2

div.head: 相關寺院
div.p: 張總記録，該址鄰近當地最早的佛教中心神通寺，高僧竺僧朗（活動於365年前後）曾修行於此。張總第36頁。早年有關神通寺的研究，見林仰山。

div.head: 相關人物
div.p: 與黄石崖1號刻經有關的供養人或群體不詳，其經主有可能爲捐造鄰近造像者。已知八則相應的造像題記，提及姓名多達八十餘人，皆爲與雕造工程有關的信眾和僧侣。其中包括兩類捐助：一類爲佛教社邑所組織的團募，此處稱“法義”（本卷黄石崖2號、3號和4號）；一類爲個體捐造（本卷黄石崖5號、6號、7號、8號和9號）。佛教社邑可能僅爲募資造像成立。比丘洪休與信眾王難生同爲35人社邑的成員，共於526年捐造彌勒像一尊（25號龕；本卷黄石崖3號刻銘）。兩人還同爲另一百餘人的社邑成員，於次年同造佛像數尊（本卷黄石崖4號）。 張總第45頁。另有兩人，乞伏鋭（本卷黄石崖6號）和姚敬遵（本卷黄石崖7號），官位居高，分别爲齊州長史與魏郡丞。頗有意思的是，兩人所捐造像皆紀年爲東魏興和元年（539）4月26日。此或爲東魏天平元年（534）北魏過渡到東魏政權後佛教活動的正式復興。早期於北魏時的捐造亦與名流大姓人家有關，其中以鄧恭伯（6世紀上半葉）與崔令姿（500－528）夫婦爲首。這些地位頗高的經主也活動於鄰近的佛教聖地，如下文討論的位於濟南東南郊的龍洞。

黄石崖3號刻銘署名“帝主元氏法義卅五人”，其中提到鄧恭伯與崔令姿夫婦。據1965年濟南東郊聖佛寺村出土東魏天平五年（538）崔令姿墓誌銘可知，崔令姿屬望族清河崔氏，武泰元年（528）卒。 王建浩和蔣寶庚。墓誌銘提及其夫婿名鄧恭伯，有“大魏征北將軍”“金紫光禄大夫”等頭銜。劉鳳君及張總認爲，乾隆版《歷城縣誌·金石考》所載濟南東南郊龍洞東魏天平四年（537）造像記提到的“征北將軍、金紫光禄大夫”可能即鄧恭伯。劉鳳君第290頁。張總第45－46頁。乾隆刻本《歷城縣誌·金石考》記録了此東魏天平四年（537）刻銘的原址。這則刻銘的著録和流存源流的簡述，見荆三林第25－26頁和張鶴雲第79頁。荆三林、張鶴雲指出，龍洞這則造像記中提到的“□州長史”乞伏鋭，與黄石崖元象二年“車騎將軍、左廣禄大夫、齊州長史、鎮城大都督、梃縣開國男乞伏鋭”造像記的像主也是同一人。 荆三林第26頁。張鶴雲第79頁。
黄石崖11號龕東魏元象二年（539）彌勒像主爲假伏波將軍、魏郡丞姚敬遵。荆三林指出，正是這些來自京都洛陽的齊州長史、車騎將軍、魏郡丞等人，將龍門的石窟造像帶到了黄石崖。 荆三林第74－75頁。此外，如上所述，乞伏鋭、鄧恭伯等人的造像活動，還將位於濟南近郊的三處北朝石窟聯繫在一起。

div.head: 斷代
div.p: 刻經無明確紀年，但根據其書法風格，應與造像同期，即北魏（386－534）晚期至東魏（534－550）早期之間。金石學家吴式芬考證更爲確切的紀年爲北魏孝昌二年（526）左右（見下節，“研究史”）。

div.head: 研究史
div.p: 黄石崖研究起步於19世紀初。然而，鮮有學者意識到該處刻經由三則佛經節文組合而成。該址因其北魏和東魏時期的山東摩崖造像而富有盛名，其造像及相應的題記在19世紀就已經引起中國金石學家的關注，而日本以及歐洲的學者也於20世紀上半葉加入。見本卷鄭岩“黄石崖──佛教石窟在山東地區的傳播”一文。張總第35－36頁，詳述20世紀20至30年代黄石崖調查狀况。張總另有簡介，見第41頁，註16。

由於周遭植被繁茂，且没有路徑通往摩崖造像，這些精緻的造像在很長一段時間被人遺忘。此經址的再次發現要歸功於清代一位叫楊恩祺的詩人及其兄弟，他們於道光元年（1821）在李惆的指引下找到此處。李惆以製作和販賣附近刻銘的拓本爲業。李金風和侯登義第62頁。楊恩祺曾作詩，題名曰 “黄石崖一百韻”。關於黄石崖的學術研究始於吴式芬。但出乎意料的是，他竟忽略了當時尚存的八則造像題記中的一則。陸增祥（1833－1889）首次完整記録並謄寫這八則録文，其調查内容收入《八瓊室金石補正》。《八瓊室金石補正》第3963－3964頁，第4235－4236頁，第4278－4279頁。這八則題記亦收録於民國十五年版《歷城縣誌》。《歷城金石續考》第395－397頁。

陶良錦在其《東魏齊州長史乞伏鋭造像記跋》詳述了一則難堪的事迹，折射出晚清收藏古代刻銘拓本的狂熱。爲此題記别緻的書法（本卷黄石崖6號）所吸引，他自言“余篤嗜魏碑，以其爲正書之始”，於光緒十六年（1890）計劃將其從岩壁鑿下，“擬移置庠中，以便購求”。工人甚至已經搭好脚手架準備開鑿，然陶良錦的行爲被張姓太守所制止。陶良錦隨後發現一個盗賣稀有拓本的商販李剩仔破壞了他的計劃，此人將陶良錦的意圖通報了張太守。陶良錦憤然指責李剩仔竊取了此刻銘，因爲光緒二十年（1894）春此刻銘即從岩石上消失。但是，刻銘拓本仍在銷售，並以陶良錦的判斷，收益相當可觀。該岩塊顯然被鑿下並碎裂成四塊。心痛之餘，陶良錦詳記拓本製作時或岩壁剥落時損毀的或遺失的字。陶良錦。
黄石崖1號的刻經抑或遭此類似命運，可能同時期被鑿下。20世紀的金石學家無人提及這件刻經。

figure.head: Upper part of niche 20, which fell off the cliff in January 1996; photograph taken in 2012
黄石崖20號龕上部，於1996年1月崩塌。2012年拍攝

div.p: 另一份記録出自20世紀20年代日本學者常盤大定與関野貞。與清代金石學家相反，這些考古學家忽視造像題記，主要關注並拍攝天然石洞中精緻的造像（2號窟），僅出版了本卷黄石崖2號、9號、4號和5號的造像題記。常盤大定與関野貞第7輯：圖版43―47。他們没有完整記録所有造像龕和題記，所以題記當時是否尚存並不明確。常盤大定與関野貞評解：第158－161頁；圖册：圖版100－104。常盤大定與関野貞第7輯：圖版43―47。英國傳教士、考古學家林仰山（F. S. Drake；1892－1974）於1936年對黄石崖作了較完整的調查。他詳盡描述所有造像龕，並拓製六處造像題記。他發佈了八則造像題記的英譯本，並根據《歷城縣誌》補充了已經佚失的兩則題記（本卷黄石崖3號和6號）。林仰山。林仰山生長於山東，中文很地道。他在職業生涯晚期，於1952年成爲香港大學的中文系教授。然而他並不知曉一篇摘録成的經文曾刻於此。

20世紀50年代，學者首次完成北魏與東魏黄石崖造像以及造像題記的詳細研究。荆三林。張鶴雲。自此，學者多次調研該址。1995年，李清泉和鄭岩對像龕重新編號（）。有關1995年的調研，見李清泉，這是關於濟南地區摩崖造像和石窟的詳盡報告，主要針對其風格展開。張總和岡田健亦參與研究，所發佈的該址地圖中，石窟編號與李清泉和鄭岩所作稍有出入。岡田健；張總。1996年1月，西邊岩壁部分塌陷。張總爲此抱憾不已。張總。2015年，李金風和侯登義發表了一篇信息豐富的文章，有相當有益的學術研究綜述。李金風和侯登義。近期相關調查由鄭岩於2016年3月所作，乃本卷“黄石崖──佛教石窟在山東地區的傳播”一文所由自。

大多數學術研究集中在黄石崖造像，極少著録刻經。唯一可知的是晚清三位金石學家的拓本和紀録。最早、最詳盡的著録出自吴式芬。出於某些原因，這些珍貴的資料並未引起重視。吴式芬精確著録了山東刻經。他對鄒城地區尖山、鐵山、崗山等刻經的考察爲後代學者研究奠定了基礎。他對黄石崖的記録包括對刻經的描述，費心地記録每行的字數，將130字悉數記録下來。見下文“討論”部分。更爲關鍵的是，他記録了已佚經文的位置： 在孝昌二年（526）的造像記下（本卷黄石崖3號），西偏，石坎。高廣與記同，當同時刻。《金石彙目分編》第21101頁。根據賴非第6－7頁，刻經高46釐米，寬50釐米，共13行，每行字數不等，每行9字左右。賴非亦提供録文。根據《歷城金石續考》第396頁，刻經爲一尺四寸高，一尺五寸寬。黄石崖1號僅存拓本，高38釐米，寬41釐米，類似近方形的黄石崖6號。不過，刻經尺寸似乎偏小。 吴式芬的著録解决了刻經的原始位置，並爲斷代孝昌二年提供有力支持。孝昌二年造像題記極有可能已與經文（本卷黄石崖1號）一同佚失。

夏曾德（活動於19世紀）編輯並於1926年出版《歷城金石續考》，也提供了經文原始位置的信息。他援引尹彭壽（1835－1904年之後）《山左六朝碑存目》：此書名並不存在，引文見於尹彭壽《山左北朝石存目》，見下文“討論”部分，。 在黄石崖最高處。昔曾見拓本，高約一尺，廣約一尺九寸。字徑約不及一寸。正書。《歷城金石續考》第397頁。 儘管這些記録使得當代學者致力尋找“黄石崖最高處”所失刻經，張總第5頁，指出未找到刻經。但不盡可靠。首先，倖存的拓本約爲方形，高38釐米，寬41釐米。但尹彭壽所記録銘寬遠遠大於銘高。其二，“黄石崖最高處”很可能爲尹彭壽誤記。

尹彭壽所撰兩種著作列出黄石崖造像題記。其《山左南北朝石刻存目》似成書較早，誤記黄石崖6號的紀年。見“討論”部分，。隨後其《山左北朝石存目》更正了此錯誤，見“討論”部分，。且添記此題位於“黄石崖最高處”。但尹氏載黄石崖1號亦位於此處，《山左南北朝石刻存目》則完全未提此刻經。陶良錦證明黄石崖6號很可能位於“黄石崖最高處”，實爲不便，以至於最後被鑿下以便近觀。因此，黄石崖1號於“黄石崖最高處”的記載極可能有誤。而據吴式芬記載，其位於黄石崖3號造像題記下方。
另一位記録此段經文的金石學家是法偉堂（1843－1907），他只稱此經爲楷書，無紀年。也許他見到的僅爲拓本，且不知此刻經崖壁的原址。
陸和九（1883－1958）所書題記僅存中國國家圖書館藏拓本，爲此刻經的另一見證者。此書1948年題記收録於本卷（黄石崖10號）。陸和九强調這份拓本的完整性，並稱岩石碎成三塊之後，另有拓本問世。他没有具體記載時間和事情始末。中國國家圖書館的拓本爲孤本，其餘拓本蕩然無存。中國國家圖書館的編輯記録該石原由清朝高官潘祖蔭（1830－1890）收藏，後佚失。北京圖書館第5册：第203頁。
由吴式芬調查可知，併合的經文應該位於西邊崖面，黄石崖3號之下。1936年，林仰山發現了由於山體滑坡或地震掉落的黄石崖3號殘石。由此推測，黄石崖1號刻經的損壞和佚失也有可能是自然災害所致，或許與黄石崖3號同時間損毀。不過，潘祖蔭曾得殘石，且斷爲三塊，似乎示意了黄石崖1號與6號相同的命運，刻意鑿下崖面以便古玩者收藏。然而，此銘文最終消失殆盡。
現今研究集中於佚失經文。1998年，張總首次確認刻經取自兩部佛經。張總第4－5頁、第41－42頁。2007年，賴非做了録文和八則造像題記，並意識到造像題記有裨於理解山東早期摩崖刻經。賴非第5－7頁。

div.head: 總目
div.p: 黄石崖的一則銘刻由取自兩部佛經的三段節文組成（黄石崖1號），據記載刻於黄石崖3號之下。此處共有八則造像題記。

div.head: 黄石崖1號
div.p: 上文研究史已提及，黄石崖1號孤拓存於北京中國國家圖書館，上有1948年陸和九題記，稱此拓乃於原石碎成三塊之前所作。另據北京中國國家圖書館編輯稱，佚失的石塊曾由潘祖蔭收藏 。他們題作“大般若涅槃經偈並造像四龕”，編號爲“善411－1”。北京中國國家圖書館第5册：第203頁。北京中國國家圖書館可能採用早期記録的標題，如夏曾德修《歷城金石續考》此書指出，尹彭壽的《山左六朝碑存目》爲研究黄石崖1號的參考文獻。或法偉堂（1843－1907）編《山左訪碑録》。《山左訪碑録》 第9055頁。但如張總所稱，此題有誤導。張總第35頁。刻銘實則爲三段取自兩部經書的段落。

第一片段爲《諸行無常偈》，見於《大般涅槃經》三個漢文譯本。此爲講述世間無常以及涅槃極樂的四字四句偈，朗朗上口，方便記憶：
諸行無常， 是生滅法。
生滅滅巳， 寂滅爲樂。《大般涅槃經》T#7，1：204c23－24。《大般涅槃經》T#374，12：450a16，451a1。《大般涅槃經》T#375，12：692a13，693a1。
此偈還刻於尖山支鍋石（尖山16.1號）。此偈語另有石刻本，見本卷。

第二段也出自《大般涅槃經》，爲五字四句偈：
如來證涅槃，永斷扵生死。
若能至心聽，常得無量樂。《大般涅槃經》T#374，12：497b9－10。《大般涅槃經》T#375，12：740c27－28。
這則驚人的偈語簡要概括了佛教修行的最終目的，即追隨終得涅槃的佛陀。此偈後來在555到560年間由僧稠（480－560）刻於一力士造像的底座，此像位於河南省安陽小南海中窟入口處右側。有關“小南海”，見。
第三段摘自《妙法蓮華經普門品》兩個漢文譯本。《妙法蓮華經》T#262，9：56c3－8。《添品妙法蓮華經》T#264，9：191b25。此段節文介紹了無盡意菩薩與佛陀的對答。主要涉及觀世音菩薩名號的功效。佛陀解釋此名號的意義在於蒼生若念此菩薩名號，菩薩即能助其脱離苦難。
在刻經佈局上，八句偈語均以空格隔開，方便讀誦。但有意思的是，第三段節文前並未留空。
這則刻於黄石崖的併合經文，屬於山東省境内最早的經刻，因而本質與後期刻經大爲不同。這則經文旨在教化，甚至勸教。它給讀者和朝聖信徒一條方便之路，即謹記佛理，並提供遇災難時清楚的指引。它簡要給出雙重承諾，一爲使眾生於來世超脱生死輪迴，二爲及時救苦救難於現世。

div.head: 黄石崖2號
div.p: 北魏正光四年（523）法義兄弟姊妹題記位於10號龕中間部分的横向方塊。方塊左上角還有黄石崖9號。這兩則願文之上，有一排四尊坐佛像，願文下方又有兩排坐佛像共五身。這九尊像顯然不是刻文裏提到的“石窟像廿四軀”。許多學者因此認爲，此則刻銘指天然洞窟中的24尊佛像和菩薩像，即刻銘東邊的2號窟。李清泉第332頁，其中提到林仰山 第199頁和荆三林。此窟造像爲此址最早的造像。林仰山指出，供養人中有外族人名，並確認他們爲統治北魏、西魏和東魏的拓跋氏。林仰山第199頁。

div.head: 黄石崖3號
div.p: 北魏孝昌二年（526）由35位佛教社邑成員所撰供養願文已失，僅存拓本。吴式芬是唯一調查黄石崖並著録全部銘文的學者。他稱此刻銘在黄石崖1號刻經之上。《金石彙目分編》第21101頁。林仰山於1936年到訪黄石崖，未見到此則刻銘，但找到西邊斷崖崩裂的石塊。石面朝下，但仍有數尊小型佛像和部分銘文可見。林仰山第197頁和第199頁。此殘石極有可能爲黄石崖3號。林仰山第202頁。崖壁殘字尚存供養人名字。1954年荆三林對此作了詳細記録。荆三林第24頁。1995年調查時，這些殘字仍在。岡田健第79頁，圖4。張總第37頁，圖1。兩者稱黄石崖3號爲刻銘G，並將殘字位置標於地圖。
35人捐造的彌勒像可能與願文同時損毀，時在19世紀下半葉或20世紀初，1936年林仰山到訪該址之前。

div.head: 黄石崖4號
div.p:
北魏孝昌三年（527）“法義兄弟一百餘人”願文位於14號與19號龕中間。刻銘區有兩則願文，一爲右半邊的黄石崖4號，一爲左邊的黄石崖5號。黄石崖4號記載石窟靈像的捐造。此爲一個大型的佛教社邑團體，因此只提及重要的社員名字。他們“上爲帝主、法界眾生”等而祈福。有關造像，僅知其爲“靈像”，其餘信息無從知曉。此則刻銘極可能和周圍所有佛龕有關。
20世紀20年代，常盤大定和関野貞到訪該址，因刻銘載“於歷山之陰，敬造石窟，彫刊靈像”，他們認爲此題記應與巨型天然山洞（2號窟）的捐造有關。常盤大定和関野貞第160頁。

div.head: 黄石崖5號
div.p: 北魏建義元年（528）王僧歡願文毗鄰黄石崖4號，提及捐造“尊像一軀”。所求功德歸於王僧歡的“七世父母、兄弟姊妹、妻子女等”。此乃個人爲其家族捐造。然而願文未談及所捐爲哪尊造像。王僧歡的捐造是在黄石崖4號完成一年後。或許，他想勝過社邑成員一百餘位“善友知識”的捐助，通過付出最高數額來爲其家人祈求更多的功德與福祉。他明確發願重生於“彌勒出世龍華三會”。

div.head: 黄石崖6號
div.p: 東魏元象二年（539）4月26日乞伏鋭造像及願文佚失已久。吴式芬到訪該址的時候，詳細記録所有刻銘，但未記此則，其中原因不明，可能因其偏僻，或藏於植被之中。晚清學者陶良錦“因履險拓工難精良”，曾試圖將此刻鑿離崖面並遷移至一處學校。 陶氏詳述碑販李剩仔如何破壞他的計劃。1894年，石刻已經消失於崖上，但拓本仍在流通，並可看出刻石已碎成四塊。陶氏認爲當時有六個刻字（2/1；3/1；4/1；5/1；6/1；7/1）完全損毀，兩個刻字（6/3；8/6）受損。現存拓本爲此證，中國國家圖書館藏繆荃孫（1844－1919）舊藏拓本A第2行到第7行的首字缺失。台北中央研究院歷史語言研究所藏有另外兩份拓本。拓本B可見六字俱在。 拓本C則比拓本A損毀更爲嚴重，不僅第2行至第7行首字缺失，第11行到第13行的首字亦不存。
刻銘的原初位置，僅見尹彭壽所記，《山左北朝石存目》第4a頁。他稱位於“黄石崖最高處”。此説或可解釋吴式芬爲何未記此處，以及陶良錦爲何急於將其鑿下。
此刻銘爲記齊州長史乞伏鋭捐造“弥勒石像一堪”。這位高官乃鮮卑人且似爲皇室重用。如其頭銜所示，車騎將軍乞伏鋭是一位高級軍事統領。他還是龍洞附近大型造像的供養人之一，其他供養人有黄石崖3號提到的鄧恭伯，以及北魏永熙三年（534）赴 任齊州刺史的汝陽王元暹。荆三林第25－26頁。張總第45－46頁。李金風和侯登義第61頁。有關更多信息，見上文“相關人物”。
現存拓本所示，該刻經址所有願文，以黄石崖6號的書法最爲精緻。擁有社會高位的供養人爲答謝脱身“賊難”而捐造佛像，刻銘爲楷書，書寫上乘，卻由此招致藏家趨之若鶩，而慘遭毀滅之災。

div.head: 黄石崖7號
div.p: 東魏元象二年（539）4月26日姚敬遵供養願文與黄石崖6號紀年相同，提到“畫飾”彌勒像一軀，應是銘文上方造像。造像爲“七世父母、現在眷屬”祈願，姚敬遵子女的名字也刊刻石上。與黄石崖5號類似，此處私人捐造意在確保供養人家屬可以優先往生净土。

div.head: 黄石崖8號
div.p: 東魏興和二年（540）銘文出自兩位居士趙勝與習仵，也屬於私人供養。供養人向彌勒佛發願，期許能往生净土面見佛陀，並祈求家人成佛。刻銘位於有四尊立佛造像的佛龕下方。其中三尊可能爲趙勝與習仵鐫造的“弥勒石像三軀”。
供養人趙勝和習仵之稱謂曾誤讀作“清信女”，但經檢視，第二行起頭有“士”字，趙勝和習仵實爲“清信士女”。

div.head: 黄石崖9號
div.p: 喬伏香題記頗爲簡短，在黄石崖2號之上，提到一尊釋迦牟尼造像，具體指哪尊造像不得而知。儘管刻銘無紀年，但應與相鄰黄石崖2號同時。《山左訪碑録》第9058頁。

div.head: 黄石崖10號
div.p: 戊子年（1948）冬十月題跋見於北京中國國家圖書館藏黄石崖1號拓本左側。題跋作者爲陸和九（1883－1958），時居北京琉璃廠慶雲堂。他稱頌這件珍拓如此完整無缺，不過又稱，近期拓本顯示岩石已斷爲三段。

div.head: 書法
div.p: 經文鐫刻中規中矩，與其他摩崖造像記無異。
就存世拓本而言，經文以楷書寫就。在北朝末年，山東地區出現了 形式多樣的刻經，字形巨大，與山體的景觀密切關聯。 就黄石崖而言，工匠們還没有形成特定的形式，以明確區分願文與經文。 這些經文與願文同刻於一處崖面上，似乎也説明當時的供養人是將刻經與造像看作同樣的功德。

div.head: Yellow Stone Cliff
div.p:
Yellow Stone Cliff (Huangshiya 黄石崖)
is the northernmost site in Shandong with engraved sutras. As it happens, it is also the earliest in date. The text is made up of three quotations from two different sutras. These were embedded in a row of niches with several dozen sculptures and their votive inscriptions. Indeed, the sculpture clusters at the site dwarfed the sutra’s single square field of script. Unfortunately, it no longer exists.

There are three more sites in Shandong where the sutra text has disappeared. But unlike those at Mount Jian 尖山, Mount Yang 陽山, and Mount Ziyang 嵫陽山, the engraved text at Yellow Stone Cliff fell victim not to quarrying but probably to acts of vandalism. This happened sometime before 1894. We know, however, of the engraved sutra through epigraphers, such as the indefatigable Wu Shifen 吴式芬 (1796–1856). One rubbing also has survived. It allows us to visualize the text, its layout, and the calligraphy.

The engraved text is not dated. It is thought to have been placed below a votive inscription to a Maitreya figure dated 526. Thus it is likely that the sutra was engraved more than three decades before the early 560s, when the next sutra texts with secure dates were cut into cliffs in Shandong. This gap in time explains the distinctive character of the Yellow Stone Cliff text, which represents a transient stage in the development of sutra engravings on stone. It was not as yet an autonomous, full-fledged text on a rocky cliff under the open sky, nor was it placed on the wall of a cave, as were sutras in compounds, such as those at Mount Xiangtang 響堂山 in Hebei, where work also began in the 560s.

The eight surviving votive inscriptions offer ample information on the donors, who were active at Yellow Stone Cliff. Among them were several highly placed individuals who were in contact with the capital, Luoyang, where, under imperial auspices, work on the monumental sculptured grottos at Longmen 龍門 had recently started.

div.head: Location
div.p: Yellow Stone Cliff is located in the southern outskirts of the provincial capital, Ji­nan City 濟南市, on the northern slope of the main peak of Mount Luosiding (Luosi­ding Shan 螺絲頂山), in the southwest of Lixia District 歷下區. Geographically, it is situated 117.0356° east and 36.6302° north (WGS84), or 503188 E (easting) and 4053851 N (northing), UTM zone 50S. Mount Luosiding, which is also called Luoyuan Monastery Peak (Luoyuan Si ding 羅袁寺頂) or Peak Luo’an Monastery (Luo’an Si ding 羅庵寺頂), is about 350 m above sea level. In the southeast, it adjoins Mount Jue­zi 撅子山, in the east, Mount Yang­tou 羊頭山 and Mount Ping­ding 平頂山. In the northwest, it is separated by a valley from Thousand Buddha Mountain (Qianfo Shan 千佛山). Along the valley runs the southern part of the municipal tourism road (fig. ). The ascent to the site is now greatly facilitated by a newly built mountain path.

Under the Northern dynasties major changes were made in the divisions of the administrative areas of today’s Jinan City. The Commentary on the Waterways Classic (Shuijing zhu: Jishui 水經注·濟水) indicates that the main part of the ancient city was in the center of today’s Ji­nan City, and that Yellow Stone Cliff was not far from the city of the date. 199–200.

div.head: Topography and Spatial Grouping
div.p: The sculptures of Yellow Stone Cliff are distributed over a steep rock face close to the peak, which descends to a terrace about 3 to 5 m in width. Lining this terrace are niches and sculpture groups carved over a horizontal stretch of rock, 35 m in length, and 10 m in height (fig. ). A natural grotto (no. 2) with sculpted images is located in the east. The small niches and sculpture groups are distributed primarily to the west of this grotto.

When a survey was undertaken in 1995, 25 small niches were still extant (fig. ), containing, together with those in the large grotto, a total of 86 Buddha and bodhisattva figures.These numbers do not include the small meditating Buddha figures and apsaras in the aureoles, nor several small Buddha figures recovered among the parts of the cliff which had collapsed. At the beginning of 1996, niches 20, 21, 22, 24 and 25, as well as a niche without any images collapsed (fig. ). Rock fragments with the remains of sculptured images can still be discovered in the ravine below the cliff.

There is no record of earlier natural rock falls, yet it is generally thought that, originally, there may have been more sculpted images. As the stone in this area of the cliff is loose, sculptures may have broken off at different times. A photo, taken by Tokiwa Daijō 常盤大定 and Sekino Tadashi 関野貞 in 1921 7: plate 43 (1). shows that the vegetation was sparse at the time; a big chunk of rock had fallen from the sculptured section, and many stones had rolled down the slope.

A composite text was carved on the cliff combining two verses from the Great Parinirvāṇa Sutra and one passage from the Lotus Sutra on the name of Bodhisattva Perceiver of the World’s Sounds (HSY 1). It measured 38 cm in height, and 41 cm in width, and had 12 columns (fig. ). This text has not survived on the rock. Wu Shifen 吴式芬 reports, in his undated Categorized Edition of an Assembled Catalog of Epigraphica (Jinshi huimu fenbian 金石彙目分編), that it was located below the votive inscription [HSY 3] of the second year of the xiaochang era (526), on the western side, where the stone is pitted. Its height and width are the same as those of the votive inscription; it must have been carved at the same time. “在孝昌二年造像記下，西偏，石坎。高廣與記同，當同時刻。”
21101.

A total of eight votive inscriptions (HSY 2–HSY 9) are known from the site. Six of them, HSY 2, HSY 4, HSY 5, HSY 7, HSY 8, and HSY 9 are still in situ (fig. and fig. ). Inscriptions HSY 3 and HSY 6 have only survived in rubbings. The votive inscription, HSY 6, of 539 by Qifu Rui 乞伏鋭 was willfully destroyed in 1892 by a certain Li Shengzai 李剩仔, who sold rubbings.See the article “Yellow Stone Cliff: Buddhist Cave Temples in Shandong” by Zheng Yan in the present volume, ; also Discussion below, p. . The rock section that had once carried HSY 3 collapsed in 1996. Reports by Zhang Zong 張總 and Okada Ken 岡田健, who had visited the site earlier, indicate that remnants were still extant on the western part of the cliff.It is inscription G in 37, fig. 1; 79, fig. 4.

div.head: Place Name
div.p: The place name Yellow Stone Cliff derives from the gleaming grey, slightly yellowish color of the Cambrian limestone that emerges in the cliff.

figure.head: Image niches 10, 11, 12 with adjacent votive inscriptions at Yellow Stone Cliff; photograph taken in 2012
黄石崖10號、11號和12號龕及其願文。2012年拍攝

div.head: Related Monasteries
div.p: Zhang Zong has noted that the site is close to Shentong Monastery 神通寺, the earliest Buddhist center in the area, where the eminent Monk Zhu Senglang 竺僧朗 (active 365) resided. 36. For the early research on Shentong Monastery see .

div.head: Related Persons
div.p: Although it is not known which individual or group of individuals was responsible for the carving of the combined sutra text HSY 1, its donor or donors may have been among those who gave the sculpted images nearby. Eight accompanying votive inscriptions are known, naming more than eighty individuals, lay people and clerics alike, who were involved in the sculpture carving projects. Two types of donations can be distinguished: Donations by communities of people organized in Buddhist associations, here named Dharma associations (fayi 法義; HSY 2, HSY 3, HSY 4), and donations by individuals (HSY 5, HSY 6, HSY 7, HSY 8, HSY 9). The named Buddhist associations may have been established for the sole purpose of collecting funds for making images. Two individuals, a certain Monk Hongxiu 比丘洪休 and a layman, Wang Nansheng 王難生, were members of the association of 35 people who donated a Maitreya figure in 526 (niche 25, HSY 3), and also members of the much larger association of more than one hundred people who donated several efficacious images one year later (HSY 4). 45. Two of the individual donors, Qifu Rui (HSY 6) and Yao Jingzun 姚敬遵 (HSY 7), occupied high official positions. They were Governors of Qi Prefecture 齊州 and Wei Commandery 魏郡, respectively. It is interesting that they date their donations to the same day, April 26, 539. This may have been the official revival of Buddhist activities at the site after the dynastic rule had changed in 534 from the Northern Wei 北魏 (386–534) to the Eastern Wei 東魏 (534–550). The earlier donations, under the Northern Wei dynasty, also involved individuals from influential families, with Deng Gongbo 鄧恭伯 (first half of the sixth century) and his wife, Cui Lingzi 崔令姿 (500–528), leading the way. These high-ranking donors were also active at neighboring Buddhist sites, such as Longdong 龍洞, located in the southeastern suburbs of Jinan, as the following discussion will now show.

The votive inscription HSY 3, donated by “thirty-five members of a Buddhist association, under the Imperial Lord of the Yuan 元 clan,” mentions Deng Gongbo and Cui Lingzi.
Cui Lingzi belonged to the powerful Cui clan of Qinghe 清河, as is known from her epitaph, dated to the fifth year of the tian­ping 天平 era of the Eastern Wei 東魏 dynasty (538), which was unearthed in 1965 at Shengfo Monastery Village (Shengfo Si cun 聖佛寺村) in the eastern suburbs of Jinan. She had died in the first year of the wutai 武泰 era (528). . The epitaph cover refers to her husband, Deng Gongbo, as “General Levying the North, Grand Master of the Palace with Golden Seal and Purple Ribbon 征北將軍、金紫光禄大夫.” Liu Fengjun 劉鳳君 and Zhang Zong 張總 think that he was the same Deng Gongbo, the “General Levying the North, Grand Master of the Palace with Golden Seal and Purple Ribbon [from here on damaged]征北將軍、金紫光禄大夫［……］,” mentioned in the now lost votive inscription, of the fourth year of the tianping era (537) of the Eastern Wei dynasty, that was formerly situated at the rear gate of Longdong in the southeastern suburbs of Jinan. 290; 45–46. The Qianlong edition Licheng Gazetteer: Epigraphic Studies (Licheng Xianzhi: jinshi kao 歷城縣誌·金石考) reports the original location of the votive inscription of 537; for a transcription of this inscription and a short summary of its fate, see 25–26 and 79. Jing Sanlin 荆三林 and Zhang Heyun 張鶴雲 make a similar connection, pointing out that the “Governor of […]zhou”, mentioned in the middle of the votive inscriptions from Longdong, is the same person as a donor at Yellow Stone Cliff named in the votive inscription of the second year of the yuanxiang 元象 era (539; HSY 6; fig. ) reading: “Chariot and Horse General, Grand Master for Splendid Happiness to the Left, Governor of Qi Prefecture, Area-commander-in-chief of Commanderies and Towns, Dynasty-founding Baron of Ting County, Qifu Rui.” “車騎將軍、左光禄大夫、齊州長史、鎮城大都督、挺縣開國男乞伏鋭。” 26; 79.

The donor of the Maitreya image in niche 11 at Yellow Stone Cliff, dated to the second year of the yuanxiang era of the Eastern Wei dynasty (539), is Yao Jingzun, Honorary General Subduing the Waves, Governor of Wei Commandery 假伏波将軍、魏郡丞 (see HSY 7). Jing Sanlin points out that Yao Jingzun came from the capital at Luoyang to take up the position of Governor of Qi Prefecture. He may have brought the practice of carving images in stone caves from Longmen to Yellow Stone Cliff. 74–75. Moreover, the names of the other high officials like the above mentioned Qifu Rui and Deng Gongbo, who may have been influential in the carving enterprise, have been found at related sites in the suburbs of Jinan.

div.head: Dating
div.p: The sutra carving had no exact date, but, on the basis of its calligraphic style, it must have been of the same period as the votive inscriptions to the sculptures, namely from the late Northern Wei 北魏 (386–534) and the early Eastern Wei 東魏 (534–550) dynasty. A more precise date, circa 526, is suggested by the observations of the epigrapher Wu Shifen (see below, Research History).

div.head: Research History
div.p: The sculpture groups at Yellow Stone Cliff have been studied since the nineteenth century. However, few scholars realized that a combination of three passages from sutras was also originally carved at the site, which is primarily well-known for the earliest cliff sculptures, dating from the Northern and Eastern Wei dynasties, in Shandong Province. These sculptures and their respective votive inscriptions attracted the attention of Chinese epigraphers of the nineteenth century, as well as of Japanese and European scholars in the first half of the twentieth century.See the article “Yellow Stone Cliff: Buddhist Cave Temples in Shandong” by Zheng Yan in the present volume, as well as 35–36, which gives a detailed report on the surveys of Yellow Stone Cliff in the 1920s and 1930s. Another summary is found in 41, note 16.

Hidden by dense vegetation and not easily accessible, because no path led up to the cliff sculptures, the finely carved images were long forgotten. A poet of the Qing dynasty, a certain Yang Enqi 楊恩祺, and his brothers, are credited with rediscovering the sculptures in 1821, guided by a man named Li Chou 李惆, who was interested in making and selling rubbings of the neighboring inscriptions. 62. Yang Enqi wrote a poem, “One Hundred Verses on Yellow Stone Cliff” (“Huangshiya yibai yun” 黄石崖一百韻). The first scholarly survey of the site was done by the reliable Wu Shifen 吴式芬, who surprisingly, however, seems to have missed one of the eight votive inscriptions that must have been still in situ at his time. The first scholar to list and transcribe all eight was Lu Zeng­xiang 陸增祥 (1833–1889) in his Supplementary Corrigenda to the Epigraphica of Eight Gems Studio (Baqiong shi jinshi buzheng 八瓊室金石補正). 3963–3964; 4235–4236; 4278–4279. The texts of the eight votive inscriptions were also transmitted in the 1926 edition of the Licheng Gazetteer (Licheng Xianzhi 歷城縣誌). 395–397.

A disturbing episode of the late Qing enthusiasm for collecting samples of ancient script was recounted by a certain Tao Liangjin 陶良錦 in his “Postscript to the Votive Inscription by the Governor of Qi Prefecture, Qifu Rui, of the Eastern Wei” (“Dong Wei Qizhou changshi Qifu Rui zaoxiangji ba” 東魏齊州長史乞伏鋭造像記跋). Drawn by the exceptional calligraphy of this votive inscription (HSY 6), this serious scholar, who said about himself that he was “earnestly dedicated to stelae inscriptions of the Wei dynasty, regarding them to be the beginning of standard script 余篤嗜魏碑，以其爲正書之始”, had, in 1890, made preparations for breaking this inscription off the cliff, to move it to a school, “in order to be able, conveniently, to meet requests for the acquisition [of rubbings] 擬移置庠中，以便購求”. Workmen had already constructed a scaffold to start chiselling, when Tao’s venture was suddenly stopped by the Prefect, Zhang. Tao later found out that his plan had been foiled by a certain Li Shengzai, a dealer in rare rubbings, who had raised the alarm with the Provincial Governor. Tao reports with indignation that Li Shengzai must later have stolen the inscription himself, because it had disappeared from the cliff by the spring of 1894. However, rubbings of it were still being sold, with considerable profit, on account of Tao’s own expertise. The stone had apparently been broken into four pieces, while being removed from the cliff. With a bleeding heart, Tao meticulously lists those characters, that were damaged or lost, when rubbings were taken or when the inscription was broken from the cliff. .
The sutra text, HSY 1, may have had a similar fate, being taken down from the cliff at that time. It is no longer mentioned by epigraphers of the twentieth century.

The next report on Yellow Stone Cliff was made by the Japanese scholars, Tokiwa Daijō 常盤大定 and Sekino Tadashi 関野貞, in the 1920s. By contrast with the epigraphers of the Qing period, these archaeologists neglected the ancient votive inscriptions and focused mainly on the finely carved sculptures inside the natural grotto (niche 2), of which they took photographs. They only published rubbings of votive inscriptions HSY 2, HSY 9, HSY 4 and HSY 5. 7: plates 43–47. Thus they did not document all niches and votive inscriptions, and it remains unclear which inscriptions were still extant at their time. Plates 100–104; Text: 158–161; 7: Plates 43–47. A more complete survey was undertaken by the British missionary and archaeologist, F. S. Drake (Lin Yangshan 林仰山; 1892–1974) in 1936. He gave comprehensive descriptions of all the niches and he also took rubbings of six votive inscriptions. He published translations of all eight votive inscriptions, supplying the text of the two inscriptions that had already been lost (HSY 3 and HSY 6) from the Licheng County Gazetteer. . Because Drake was born and raised in Shandong, it is likely that he spoke Chinese as a native. Later in his career, in 1952, he became Professor of Chinese at the University of Hong Kong. However, he was not aware that a combination of sutra texts had once been carved at the site.

In the 1950s, scholars produced the first comprehensive studies of the Wei sculptures and the adjacent votive inscriptions at Yellow Stone Cliff. ; . From then on, the site has been surveyed more than once. In 1995, Li Qingquan 李清泉 and Zheng Yan 鄭岩 gave new numbers to the niches (fig. ).For the survey of 1995, see , which is a comprehensive study of all cliff sculptures and caves in the Jinan region, focussing on issues of style. Zhang Zong 張總 and Okada Ken 岡田健 also participated in these surveys; they published site maps with a numbering of the caves differing slightly from that made by Li Qingquan and Zheng Yan. ; . In January 1996, part of the western cliff section collapsed, and, following this, Zhang Zong looked back at Yellow Stone Cliff with great regret. . In 2015, Li Jinfeng 李金風 and Hou Deng­yi 侯登義 published a substantial article on Yellow Stone Cliff, with a helpful summary of all academic work up to that time. . The most recent examination, undertaken in March 2016, has served as the basis for the article by Zheng Yan in the present volume, “Yellow Stone Cliff: Buddhist Cave Temples in Shandong”.

While most academic effort has focused on the early sculptures at Yellow Stone Cliff, the carved sutra passage is less well documented. It is only known from a surviving rubbing and the accounts by three epigraphers of the late Qing dynasty. The earliest and most detailed survey of the sutra passage, HSY 1, was made by Wu Shifen. For what­ever reason, this valuable source has been generally overlooked. Wu Shifen records the Shandong stone scriptures accurately. His work in the region of Zoucheng, at Mounts Jian, Tie 鐵山, and Gang 崗山, provided a solid foundation for successive generations of scholars. His account on Yellow Stone Cliff includes a description of the carved sutra passage, in which he painstakingly counts the numbers of characters in each column, arriving at the correct total of 130 characters.See Discussion. Even more important is his note on the original position of the now lost sutra passage, which was once
div.quote: located below the votive inscription [HSY 3] of the second year of the xiaochang 孝昌 era [526], on the western side, where the stone is pitted. Its height and width are the same as those of the votive inscription; it must have been carved at the same time. 21101. According to 6–7, this inscription measures 46 cm in height, and 50 cm in width. It has 13 columns, with an uneven number of characters, about nine in each column. Lai Fei also gives a transcription. According to 396, this inscription measures 1 foot 尺, 4 inches 寸 in height, and 1 foot, 5 inches in width. The rubbing of HSY 1 measures 38 cm in height, 41 cm in width, comparable with the votive inscription, which was almost square. However, it seems to have been slightly smaller in size.
div.p: This observation solves the question of the original location of the engravings, and it provides a good argument to support a date of 526. As the votive inscription of 526 (HSY 3) is lost, it is possible that it and the sutra passage (HSY 1) disappeared together.

The Sequel to the Investigation of Epigraphica of Licheng (Licheng jinshi xukao 歷城金石續考), edited by Xia Zengde 夏曾德 (nineteenth century) and published in 1926, also provides information on the original location of the sutra passage. Xia Zengde quotes a work by Yin Pengshou 尹彭壽 (1835 – after 1904), with the title Catalog of Extant Stelae from the Six Dynasties in Shandong (Shanzuo liuchao bei cunmu 山左六朝碑存目),A work with this title is not known; but the quoted passage appears in Yin Pengshou’s Catalog of Extant Stones from the Northern Dynasties in Shandong (Shanzuo beichao shi cunmu 山左北朝石存目), see below, Discussion, p. . who says:
div.quote: [According to Yin Pengshou, HSY 1] is located at the highest point on Yellow Stone Cliff. I have once seen a rubbing. It measured about 1 foot in height and about 1 foot and 9 inches in width. The diameter of a single character was on average less than 1 inch. Regular script. 397.
div.p: Although this account has made contemporary scholars look for the lost sutra inscription “at the highest point on Yellow Stone Cliff”, 5, found nothing. it does not seem reliable. First, the surviving rubbing is roughly square in size (38 x 41 cm), while Yin Peng­shou says that its width was considerably larger than its height. Secondly, Yin Pengshou’s record that HSY 1 was on “the highest point on Yellow Stone Cliff 黄石崖最高處” may have been mistaken.

Yin Pengshou listed the votive inscriptions of Yellow Stone Cliff in two of his works. His Catalog of Extant Stone Inscriptions from the Northern and Southern Dynasties in Shandong (Shanzuo Nanbeichao shike cunmu 山左南北朝石刻存目) seems to have been written first, because here, he recorded HSY 6 with a wrong date.See below, Discussion, p. . Later, he corrected this mistake in his Catalog of Extant Stones from the Northern Dynasties in Shandong (Shanzuo beichao shi cunmu 山左北朝石存目).See below, Discussion, p. . Only then did he add that the inscription, HSY 6, was located “at the highest point of Yellow Stone Cliff.” However, he ascribes the same position also to the gatha of the Great Parinirvāṇa Sutra (HSY 1), which had not been mentioned at all in his earlier work, the Catalog of Extant Stone Inscriptions from the Northern and Southern Dynasties in Shandong. Tao Liangjin provides evidence that the votive inscription HSY 6 may indeed have been placed very inconveniently “at the highest point of Yellow Stone Cliff,” with the consequence that to make it accessible it was broken off the cliff. Thus the suggestion that the combined sutra passage, HSY 1, was located “at the highest point of Yellow Stone Cliff” was probably a mistake. According to Wu Shifen’s record, it was placed below another votive inscription, HSY 3.

Another epigrapher who took note of the sutra passage is Fa Weitang 法偉堂 (1843–1907); but as he only says that it was written in standard script, and that it is undated, he may have simply seen a rubbing, without knowing where it was placed on the cliff.

The colophon by Lu Hejiu 陸和九 (1883–1958), written on the only surviving rubbing in the National Library of China in Beijing, is another witness to the fate of the sutra passage. This colophon, dated to 1948, has been transcribed in the catalog section of the present volume (HSY 10). Lu Hejiu emphasises the integrity of this particular rubbing; more recent rubbings, he says, had been taken after the stone had broken into three pieces. However, he does not mention when and how this happened. The rubbing in the National Library of China in Beijing is the only one to survive, and no other rubbings that might show the shattered parts are known. The National Library of China editors note that the stone was in the possession of the high Qing official, Pan Zuyin 潘祖蔭 (1830–1890), but is now lost.See 5: 203.

As is known from Wu Shifen’s survey, the combined sutra passage must have been located in the western cliff section, below HSY 3. In 1936, Drake had found fragments of HSY 3 on a rock that had fallen from the cliff due to natural causes like landslides or earthquakes. Therefore, it is possible that the fracturing and final loss of the stone with the sutra passages, HSY 1, may also have been due to natural causes, perhaps even contemporary with the loss of HSY 3. However, the fact that the stone came into the possession of Pan Zuyin, and also broke into three pieces, may just as well mean that HSY 1 shared the same fate as HSY 6—it may have been chiselled off the cliff intentionally to join the collection of some antiquarian, before it finally disappeared from the scene.

Recent research has focused on the lost sutra passages. In 1998, Zhang Zong was the first to identify them correctly as a combination of quotations taken from two separate sutras. 4–5, 41–42. In giving transcriptions of the combined sutra passage as well as of the eight votive inscriptions, Lai Fei realized in 2007 that the adjacent votive inscriptions might provide a basis for a better understanding of Shandong’s earliest polished cliff sutra carving. 5–7.

div.head: Inventory
div.p: At Yellow Stone Cliff, three passages from two sutras are combined in one inscription (HSY 1). Allegedly it was carved below votive inscription HSY 3. There were altogether eight votive inscriptions.

div.head: HSY 1
div.p: As mentioned in the Research History, the only rubbing of HSY 1 is in the National Library of China. It has the colophon of 1948 by Lu Hejiu, who states that the rubbing was taken before the stone was broken into three pieces. The editors in the library note that, the now lost stone was once in the possession of Pan Zuyin. They use the title “The Gatha [on Impermanence] from the Great Parinirvāṇa Sutra and four image niches”, cataloged as shan 善 411-1.See 5: 203. The Library may have adopted this title from earlier records, such as the Sequel to the Investigation of Epigraphica of Licheng by Xia Zeng­deThis work refers to a Catalog of Extant Stone Inscriptions from the Six Dynasties in Shandong by Yin Pengshou as a source for HSY 1. or the Record of Enquiries into Stelae in Shandong (Shanzuo fangbei lu 山左訪碑録) by Fa Weitang (1843–1907). 9055. Yet, as Zhang Zong has pointed out, the title is misleading. 35. The carved passage consists of three sections culled from two sutras, which were then combined into a new text.

The first section, also known as the “Verse of Impermanence of All Conditioned Phenomena” (zhuxing wuchang ji 諸行無常偈), is found in three Chinese translations of the Great Parinirvāṇa Sutra (Da banniepan jing 大般涅槃經). Its four times four characters condense the fundamental Buddhist teaching of samsaric impermanence and blissful nirvana in a cogent way, so that it can be easily remembered:
div.quote: All conditioned phenomena are impermanent; this is the Law of birth and extinction. After birth and extinction have been extinguished, tranquil extinction is bliss. 諸行无常， 是生滅法； 生滅滅巳， 寂滅爲樂。T#7, 1: 204c23–24; T#374, 12: 450a16, 451a1; T#375, 12: 692a13, 693a1.
div.p: This verse was later also carved at Mount Jian, on Pot Stand Rock (JS 16.1).For other carved versions of the verse, see above, .
The second section is another verse from the Nirvana Sutra, in four times five characters:
div.quote: When the Thus Come One witnessed nirvana, he forever cut off life and death; whoever can understand [this] with a perfected mind, will in perpetuity obtain immeasurable bliss. 如来證涅槃，永断扵生死；若能至心聴，常淂無量樂。 T#374, 12: 497b9–10; T#375, 12: 740c27–28.
div.p: This equally striking formulation contains in a nutshell the ultimate goal of all Buddhist practice, namely to follow the Buddha, who has obtained nirvana. This verse was later carved by the eminent monk Sengchou 僧稠 (480–560) between 555–560 on the pedestal of the right hand guardian figure in front of the entrance to the middle cave at Xiaonanhai 小南海 near Anyang 安陽, Henan Province.For Xiaonanhai see above, .

The third section of the inscription is a passage in prose from the “Chapter on the Universal Gate” (“Pumen pin” 普門品) in two translations of the Sutra of the Lotus of the Wonderful Law (Miaofa lianhua jing 妙法蓮華經).T#262, 9: 56c3–8; T#264, 9: 191b25. It introduces one question asked by Bodhisattva Akṣayamati and the Buddha’s response to it. The subject of the question is the name of the Bodhisattva Perceiver of the World’s Sounds. The Buddha explains that the meaning of this name is that this bodhisattva will safeguard all suffering beings, and help them attain liberation from their sufferings as soon as his name is invoked.

In the layout of the engraved text, all eight verses are separated by empty spaces, so that they can be easily memorized and recited, but, interestingly, there is no space before the beginning of the third section.

This combined sutra text carved at Yellow Stone Cliff, the earliest sutra carving in the whole of Shandong, is thus very different in character from later sutra carvings. It seems explicitly pedagogical, even proselytizing, and it provides the reader or pilgrim with a ready way to remember an outline of the Buddhist teaching, and an equally clear instruction of what to do when in distress. In concise form, it gives a twofold promise, one of liberation from the circle of life and death in the long run, and one of assistance in worldly sufferings right now and here.

div.head: HSY 2
div.p: The votive inscription of 523 by “the brothers and sisters of the Buddhist association” is located in a horizontal field of script in the center of niche 10. The upper left corner of this field of script also contains HSY 9. Above the two votive inscriptions, four meditating Buddha figures are aligned; below are five meditating Buddha figures in two rows. These nine figures obviously are not the “24 stone cave images 石窟像廿四軀” mentioned in this inscription. Many scholars therefore believe that the inscription refers to the 24 Buddha and bodhisattva figures inside the natural grotto, cave 2, located east of the inscription. 332, with references to 199 and . It follows that the figures in the grotto are the earliest at the site. Drake already noted the foreign family names among the donors, which he identified as belonging to the Tuoba 拓跋, who were the rulers of the Northern, Western, and Eastern Wei dynasties. 199.

div.head: HSY 3
div.p: The votive inscription of 526 by 35 members of a Buddhist Association is now lost but preserved in rubbings. Wu Shifen was the only person who was able to record it in its entirety, when he visited Yellow Stone Cliff. He says that it was located above the carved sutra passages of HSY 1. 21101. When Drake explored Yellow Stone Cliff in 1936, he could not find this inscription, but he found a rock that had broken off from the western part of the cliff. The rock was lying upside down and carried the remains of a few small Buddhist figures and parts of an inscription. 197, 199. This inscription was probably HSY 3. Fragments, with donor names, were still visible higher up on the cliff. 202. They were documented in detail by Jing Sanlin in 1954. 24. During the survey of 1995, such fragments were still located on the western part of the cliff. 79, fig. 4 and 37, fig. 1, who designate HSY 3 as inscription G, indicate the position of the fragments on their site map.

The Maitreya figure donated by the 35 members of a Buddhist association, was probably lost at the same time as the votive inscription, either already in the second half of the nineteenth century, or at the beginning of the twentieth century, before 1936, when Drake visited the site.

div.head: HSY 4
div.p: The votive inscription of 527 by “more than one hundred brethren of the Buddhist association 法義兄弟一百餘人” is located in the center of the cluster of niches 14–19. The field of script is shared by two votive inscriptions, HSY 4 in the right half, and HSY 5 in the left half. HSY 4 reports the donation of stone caves 石窟 with efficacious images 靈像. This is a donation by a large community, naming only its most important members, for the sake of the entire empire, and all living beings in the Dharma Realm. No further details about the donated niches or images are given, apart from their characterization as “efficacious”. The inscription probably refers to all the surrounding niches.

When Tokiwa Daijō 常盤大定 and Sekino Tadashi 関野貞 visited the site in the 1920s, they thought that this inscription might refer to the donation of sculptures inside the large grotto (niche 2), as it speaks about “respectfully making stone caves at the north side of Mount Li and having efficacious images carved 於歷山之陰，敬造石窟，彫刊靈像.” 160.

div.head: HSY 5
div.p: The votive inscription of 528 by Wang Senghuan 王僧歡, adjacent to HSY 4, talks about the donation of one figure of an honored image 尊像一軀. The merit thus received is dedicated, among others, to “the ancestors of seven generations, the brothers and sisters, wife and children 七世父母、兄弟姊妹、妻子女等” of the main donor, Wang Senghuan. It is a donation by an individual for the sake of his family. The inscription does, however, not say to which of the surrounding images it refers. Wang Senghuan, who accomplished his donation one year after that by the more than one hundred brethren, may have been affiliated with this large Buddhist association—he speaks of “virtuous friends and fellow practitioners 善友知識”—and may have hoped to secure more blessings for his own family by raising a larger fund than all other individual members of the association. He explicitly states his wish for rebirth “when Maitreya appears in the world, among the three assemblies under the Dragon Flower [tree] 彌勒出世龍華三會”.

div.head: HSY 6
div.p: The stone with the votive inscription of April 26, 539 by Qifu Rui was lost a long time ago. When Wu Shifen visited the site, he carefully documented all inscriptions, apart from this one. The reasons why he missed it are not known. It is possible that it was inaccessible or hidden by vegetation. From the account by Tao Liangjin, a scholar of the late Qing dynasty, we know that he had intended to break it off the cliff and move it to a school, as “on account of the danger faced by workers who make rubbings, it is difficult to achieve high quality 因履險拓工難精良”. Tao narrates in detail how his plan was foiled by the dealer in rubbings, Li Shengzai. In 1894, the inscription was no longer on the cliff, but rubbings were still in circulation, showing that it had been broken into four pieces. Tao says that six characters (2/1; 3/1; 4/1; 5/1; 6/1; 7/1) were destroyed in the process, and two more (6/3; 8/6) had been damaged.

The surviving rubbings lend credibility to this account: On rubbing A, now in the National Library of China, but formerly in the collection of Miao Quansun 繆荃孫 (1844–1919), the first characters in columns 2–7 are missing. However, the Institute of History and Philology at the Academia Sinica in Taibei possesses two more rubbings of this inscription. On rubbing B, these six characters are still extant. Rubbing C, on the other hand, is even more damaged than rubbing A. Here, not only the first characters in columns 2–7 are no longer there, but characters 11/1, 12/1, and 13/1 are also missing.

The only information on the original location of the inscription is from Yin Pengshou, 4a. who said that it was situated “at the highest point on Yellow Stone Cliff 黄石崖最高處”. This would explain why Wu Shifen missed it, and why Tao Liangjin was eager to break it off the cliff.

The inscription commemorates the donation of “a stone image of Maitreya in a niche 弥勒石像一堪” by the Governor of Qi Prefecture, Qifu Rui. This high-ranking official was of Xianbei 鮮卑 origin and seems to have enjoyed the particular trust of the imperial house. As indicated by his title, Chariot and Horse General, Qifu Rui had a high military rank. In addition, he was one of the donors of another large image in nearby Longdong, together with Deng Gongbo, named in HSY 3, and the Prince of Ruyang 汝陽王, Yuan Xian 元暹, who had from 534 been Regional Chief of Qi Prefecture 齊州刺史. 25–26; 45–46; 61. For more information, see above, Related Persons.

As the surviving rubbings show, HSY 6 has the finest calligraphy of all votive inscriptions at the site. The high social status of the donor, his vow to make images to repay the mercy for having once been saved from robbers, and the inscription’s fine execution in standard script may be the reasons why it was coveted by collectors, an appreciation that finally resulted in its destruction.

div.head: HSY 7
div.p: This votive inscription by an individual donor, Yao Jingzun, is dated to April 26, 539. The date is identical to that of HSY 6. It reports the carving and painting of one Maitreya image, which must be the image directly above. As beneficiaries are named the “ancestors of seven generations, and the family members in this life 七世父母、現在眷屬;” names of Yao Jingzun’s children are also given. Like HSY 5, this is a private donation to secure favoured rebirths for the family.

div.head: HSY 8
div.p: The votive inscription of 540 by two lay people, Zhao Sheng 趙勝 and Xi Wu 習仵, is another private donation. The donors set their hopes on Buddha Maitreya, whom they wish to encounter face-to-face in future rebirths, and they pray that all family members may achieve Buddhahood. The inscription is located below a niche containing four standing Buddha figures. Three of these may be the “stone images of Maitreya 弥勒石像三軀” donated by Zhao Sheng and Xi Wu.

On account of the title, which has been misread as a “women of pure faith” (qingxin nü 清信女), the donors, Zhao Sheng and Xi Wu, have both been thought to be women. However, an investigation of the stone clearly shows that there is another character in the beginning of column 2, reading “[gentle]man” (shi 士), so that Zhao Sheng and Xi Wu were actually a “man and woman of pure faith” (qingxin shi nü 清信士女).

div.head: HSY 9
div.p: The short, votive inscription by Qiao Fu­xiang 喬伏香 is located above HSY 2. It reports the donation of one image of Śākyamuni; however, it is unclear to which sculpture this refers. Although the inscription is undated, it has been suggested that it dates to about the same time as the neighboring inscription HSY 2. 9058.

div.head: HSY 10
div.p: This colophon, dated to the tenth month in the winter of a wuzi 戊子 year (1948), was written in ink to the left of the rubbing of HSY 1 in the National Library of China in Beijing. The author is Lu Hejiu 陸和九 (1883–1958), a resident of Auspicious Clouds Hall (Qingyun Tang 慶雲堂) in Liulichang 琉璃廠 in Beijing. He extols the rare rubbing, which shows the inscription still intact. He further comments that more recent rubbings would, by contrast, reveal that the stone had already been broken into three pieces.

div.head: Calligraphy
div.p: The carving of the sutra text is straight and narrow, like many of the votive inscriptions on the cliff face.

As the surviving rubbing shows, the sutra texts were written in standard script. During the late Northern dynasties period, sutra inscriptions in manifold shapes appeared in the Shandong region in an intimate relation with the mountain landscape, some in huge characters. At Yellow Stone Cliff, the artisans had not yet developed a particular form for sutra texts, distinct from votive inscriptions. As these sutra texts were carved together with the votive inscriptions, it seems that the donors of the day regarded the merit derived from sutra texts and from sculptures as equivalent.
